title: Autobiography of Benjamin Franklin Book Synopsis - Request

transcript.text: Hello, this is a video request. The autobiography of Benjamin Franklin. So I'll do, I'll talk about the autobiography and also Benjamin Franklin from what I read on the Wikipedia of he and his book.

I listened to it today on Kindle. It's not available on Books Should Be Free where people read it. At least not as of two years ago when I collected it. I like listening to stuff on Kindle because you can listen to it at double speed so you can get through the book at half, you know, in half the time.

And the voices that it reads with are pretty good. I usually try to listen to it with a girl voice, because I ain't no gay. But I listened to it with a dude voice and it was really good, man, today. I liked it.

But, uh, um, it's about 69,000 words. I got through it at about, um, three hours or something. I, I listened to it and then I went on a run and I came back and then I was riding my bike for like a half hour on the trainer here until it was done.

So, um, so, um, uh, so the autobiography of Benjamin Franklin, it was written between 17, nine, 71 and 90. Um, so, uh, so it's unfinished. Um, and, uh, it ended, um, when he was writing, it didn't end in 1790.

It ended in, um, uh, like during the war with, um, um, America and France or England and France. Um, and it began with his, his, his, uh, childhood. So I'll just, uh, re, um, recall, I'll just talk about what I recall on it.

Um, in the very beginning, he's talking about his, um, his, um, lineage. Uh, he said he was the, for five generations in a row, he was the youngest of the family. So he's, he's the youngest of his father. They're like, his father had like 17 kids over three wives, but his father was the youngest of his father.
He was the youngest for five generations. And, um, uh, his, on his mother's side, there were the Folgers. They came over at the very beginning of America with the Puritans.
And, uh, her father was like a noted, you know, um, proponent of free speech. The Puritans were the, uh, the super, uh, religious people in England who, uh, were very, um, devout and, um, austere.

Like they, they believed in, uh, you know, it was kind of, um, uh, where people would just, you know, live simply and be, live responsibly and not try to be, not try to, uh, put your values on making money, but just living a good life.
And, uh, and, uh, so they took a lot of stuff, you know, like really far, like, uh, that's why Americans are like against nudity and, uh, not very sexual, uh, kind of, uh, a, uh, holdover from that.
Uh, and his father, um, was, uh, poor. He grew up poor. Uh, his father was a, uh, candle maker and employed Benjamin Franklin as a candle maker.
But then when he was a kid, he went to work for his older brother, James, uh, his, uh, who was a, um, working as a printer.

And, uh, so he was under him as a, uh, um, like a, um, like an indentured servant, you know, where you would have to work under a contract to pay for your, uh, your own education and become a tradesman.
And, uh, during that time he started writing, um, articles in, in his, uh, brother's, uh, newspaper under a false name.
And, uh, I guess his brother then found out about it and got angry with him for doing that.
And they had a falling out because his brother had, I guess, been previously jailed for writing things, jailed by the English.
They didn't have a whole lot of, um, freedom of speech back then.
And maybe he was, uh, worried about his brother's, um, you know, safety or something like that.
But he had a falling out and then, so he just quit before his indentured servitude was up and ran away to another town.
And, uh, at seven, at the age of 17 and, um, and, uh, tried to get a job with other printers over there, but was having trouble getting, uh, work.
But, uh, eventually did get a job as a, uh, I think he moved to Pennsylvania.
That's where he spent most of his life.
And eventually got a job as a, um, as a, uh, yeah, as a printer and started writing pamphlets under a pseudonym.
Um, um, um, I guess people, like, felt like they were more, uh, more, uh, safe, safer to do stuff under a pseudonym.
Um, and also, uh, you know, maybe they just didn't, uh, they thought doing stuff to try to get recognition wasn't, uh, the honorable thing to do.
So, and, uh, he, Benjamin Franklin is one of the founding fathers of America, one of the most famous Americans.
Let me just talk about Benjamin Franklin a little bit and then I'll, and I'll continue with what I remember of his autobiography.
But he, uh, you know, along with, like, um, Washington, Jefferson, John Adams, Benjamin Franklin.
Those are probably the four, you know, most popular, most, most famous ones, you know, or I guess the top three, you know, Jefferson, Washington, and Franklin.
So Franklin, of all the founding fathers, he was the only, the one, the most famous one who was never the president.
And some people called him like the, the first, like the, the only president of America who wasn't actually president.
But he was so famous because he, he was a polymath.
He did lots of different things.
He invented lots of stuff.
He's most, um, noted, well, he's most famous, I guess, for his, um, like in, in the world, in the political world, he was the, uh, ambassador of America to France.
And, um, the ambassador also to, uh, Sweden, even though he never went there.
And so he was, he went to France to try to, um, uh, make the French like America and support America and their war against England.
And, uh, France did donate a lot of money and ships and, uh, helped America win.
I don't know if America would have won without the aid of France.
And that was actually one of the things that brought France down because they were spending too much money helping America when they weren't helping their own people.
But he spent, um, he was really famous in, in, uh, Europe and France because of his, um, his scientific work.
When he was younger, he did a lot of scientific work.
And then when he was older, he was, uh, he, um, became more involved in politics.
But he was just, he was really famous in, in Europe as a philosopher and hung out with, uh, Voltaire and, uh, lots of also, um, English philosophers.

Before he went to France, he was an ambassador to England and was in England for five years, um, trying to, um, make, um, trying to change laws because the, the pens in Pennsylvania, they were kind of living off of the fat of the people over there.
And, uh, he thought that wasn't fair and they were, he was kind of like, uh, um, they were like leeches.
And so he was trying to take their power away from them because Pennsylvania originally was, uh, was belonged to a guy named William Penn and, uh, it wasn't, didn't even belong to the king.
He was like, he was like deeded over to him.
And then his, his, uh, his descendants were, uh, kind of, kind of little mini kings over there.
And so he went over to try to, um, try to get, make it, make it a royal colony.
But I guess that was, um, that was one of his regrets, I think.
But, um, because he, you know, they were, later on he was, he fought for independence from England.
But, um, but he was known, known in Europe as a, uh, the most famous, which made him really famous in Europe was his discovery of electricity.
That's probably his most famous, um, uh, claim to fame.
Um, he, by, and I think everybody knows this story, how he flew the kite up in the rain, in the, in the electrical storm with the key hanging from it.
And then he let the wire get, the, uh, the kite cord get drenched in water and then the, the lightning would hit it and it would, the electricity would come down.
So he wrote theories about that and he invented the lightning rod.
So then they put, he put a lightning rod on one of, um, on the, uh, university he started.
I mean, he had his hands in all sorts of stuff.
He did all sorts of stuff.
He started a first, um, I think university in Philadelphia, which was like the first university in America.
He started the first public library in all of America.
They were called subscription libraries.
I guess donors would give money or something.
And, uh, because he thought that, you know, people deserved, uh, every, every man deserved education.
And, uh, he invented, um, he invented, uh, a glass harmonica that you could play music by, by moving the glass inside of something as you, as you kept blowing in.
I don't know if it's still in existence, but it made it to England.
And, um, he, um, he also came up with lots of, uh, different, uh, scientific, um, ideas.
Um, maybe let's see if we can just list off all the inventions because he did a lots of stuff.
Um, um, um, do, do, do, do, do, do, um, he did, uh, he, I can't remember.
And, uh, oh, well, he, he was the first person to, uh, try to describe the, um, the Gulf Stream.
And I think he even coined the term Gulf Stream because he noticed that ships took like weeks longer to get to, uh, New York than, than it did to, to Rhode Island.
And so just, and so he proposed that they would just go take another route, the same route that they did to Rhode Island.
And he chopped off the, uh, the, how long it would take by two weeks.
And, um, yeah, he just was nonstop.
He did all sorts of stuff.
He wrote, when he was 20 years old, he wrote, uh, like he had 13, um, um, like he made his own, like 13 commandments for himself.
That he would follow, and he would try to follow one each week.
Um, and, uh, which you can see on his Wikipedia page.
And, um, he was one of the first, um, to believe in wave theory, that life traveled, light traveled as a wave.
And, um, yeah, so he was just an all-around genius.
And, uh, and when he went to America, you can see a good depiction of him and, um, how he was in, uh,
That miniseries, John Adams, starring, uh, Paul Giamatti.
That English guy was playing Benjamin Franklin, but he was over there, like, hanging out in Versailles
With all of the, uh, the French aristocracy.
And he always, he always wore, uh, one of those Davy Crockett hats.
The, the, like, uh, you know, like a skin over his head.
Because he was known as, like, the, the, the rustic American philosopher.
And, um, you know, like he was, which the, which the French, even the aristocracy really liked.
And, uh, um, yeah, he was, like, in the center of everything.
They should have a, make a movie about him.
Because he was, he was, he experienced a lot of, uh, the epicenter of, uh, of a lot of action going on.
He was, uh, one of the original, uh, like, um, writers of the Constitution.
And his original idea, like on our dollar, we have our motto.
We have, like, in God we trust.
His, his idea, original idea was, um, um, to fight against tyranny and trust in God.
Which is cool.
Um, yeah, so, uh, so, um, what I remember from his autobiography is, uh, he, um, he went over and he, he was a printer.
And he called himself a printer.
When he wrote stuff, he called himself a printer instead of, like, uh, like, you know, Dr. Franklin.
Because he was given, um, two honorary degrees from Harvard and Yale.
And, uh, so he wasn't very pretentious.
Um, and, um, he, uh, yeah, he just, when some of the things I remember him saying is, um, he liked to swim.
He would, he did some big swim.
And I, I want to Google it and see where he swam.
Because it seemed like he was bragging about it.
From, like, one place to another place.
And he, you know, he didn't know, like, he only knew a couple of other people who did it.
And, um, he, uh, he had loaned money to a lot of, to a few people and never got paid back.
So he seemed like, uh, kind of like a little bit over-trusting.
He had one, and, and he wasn't paid back a lot of time because people were drunk.
He, he complained that a lot of people who he interacted with were drunk all the time.
He had one friend who he loaned a lot of money to who, uh, who was, they were rowing a boat, like, across the river going somewhere.
And his friend was referring, refusing to take his turn rowing and was drunk.
And, uh, Benjamin Franklin was, was like, he was, he was, he was a leader as he was a kid.
So he was like, you know, your turn to row.
And the guy said, no way, I'm not going to row.
I'm going to come up and kick you.
Stop telling me to row.
So he punched him in the balls.
Um, what he said, he clapped him in the balls.
I don't know what that means.
But then threw him over the boat.
And then, and then the guy would try to get it back in the boat and they'd row away until he'd say, okay, you can't come in the road and boat unless you row.
And then I was like, no way, I will never row.
And they finally had to pull him back in the boat.
And even though he refused to row.
And then he like, so after that, they weren't, they were like enemies and he never got his money back.
And he loaned money to us like some, and I think he said like, it was like, it was like a good riddance to him.
You know, at least I don't have to be his friend anymore because he doesn't, he doesn't pay me back.
So maybe it's worth it.
But, uh, but there are other people, other examples like that.
He mentioned another one of his alcoholic friends who, who had like a pint when he woke up, a pint for brunch, a pint for lunch, um, you know, a pint in the afternoon and a pint at night.
And the guy justified it because there were calories.
But Benjamin, but he told his friend like, well, there's just as many calories as in a, you know, a one pence loaf of bread.
Why don't you just eat that bread and then drink water?
And then you won't have to, it's a lot cheaper and you don't have to, uh, be drunk all the time.
Um, I don't think his friend liked that very much.

Um, but, um, if you listen to a lot of those historical books, uh, they all like around in those days and talking about poor people, they always, they always say that like the poor class is always, they're all drunks.
And like alcohol is the big evil in the world and just destroys people.
Um, but, uh, so, and yeah, so then he was just, uh, he moved, um, he was, he, uh, he worked on like some different newspapers.
He worked on a, uh, German language newspaper, but I guess it failed.
And then he worked on a, he started a, uh, American newspaper, I guess in Philadelphia, something that made him money and was doing really well.
And, um, and so he, um, just, he was, uh, well known for all of his, his hard work.
He was a really hard worker.
Um, I remember listening to what his ideas about stuff were, uh, one of his, like his grandfather believed in transmigration, which in those days is called a reincarnation.
So, um, and he was never religious.
He, he called himself a deist, but not a Christian.
So he was, he was like a hippie in those days.
He had long hair.
He was a vegetarian, um, because he didn't want to hurt fish.
Imagine that back in the 1700s when the violent, in those violent days, he, he didn't want to eat fish because he didn't want to kill them.
But then he was eating fish one day or watching his friends eat fish and they saw a little fish inside the bellies of the big fish.
And then he said, okay, well, if those fish don't have problems eating each other, I guess I can eat them too.
It's okay for me to eat them.
So then he became a, a fish eater after that.
But, um, so yeah, he was, uh, he was like a free thinking hippie.
He, um, he was a deist because he didn't like when he was younger.
Cause he didn't like, um, he didn't think people should like follow, um, rules, um, and, uh, you know, be like blind followers of religion.

And when he was in France, he talked, um, King, uh, Louis the 16th into abolishing, um, into, um, making a decree that, uh, allowed for religious freedom so that people could open, didn't have to be Catholic.
And they couldn't, they could openly be another religion.
Um, and, um, he, he never went to church.
He said on Sundays, he, uh, that was his day of study.

And, uh, uh, so he didn't need to, you know, be reminded of, of God through church, but he said he believed that our souls were immortal and that, um, there is a, like a higher being out there that controls the world that, that rewards good deeds and punishes bad deeds in this world or in the hereafter.
But I think later in his life, he said that, uh, organized religion is good for keeping people good.
And, uh, and, uh, I guess so became a Christian because of that when he was older, but, uh, he died at the age of 84.
So he lived a long time.
He was, uh, kind of obese though.
He, I think he, um, eventually died of something related to like gout or, but I mean, 84 is pretty old.
He'd lived a pretty nice long time.
Um, he had a son who was an illegitimate son and nobody knows who his mother was, but he had a falling out with a son cause he was a loyalist.
And when America lost the colonies or the England lost the colonies, he moved to, uh, his son moved to England and never came back.
But then his son's son, uh, lived in America or moved to America or, or like met Benjamin Franklin in England.
And he kind of adopted him, um, um, and, and took him to France with him as like his, uh, stenographer or something.
Um, um, and, uh, um, he, uh, he tried to, he proposed to, he, uh, I think it's one of his wives.
I think he was married like twice, uh, his first wife, he proposed to her and I guess her mother didn't allow, allow it cause he wasn't wealthy enough.
Um, and, but she married some other guy who took the dowry and then escaped to Barbados.
And so he just became common law marriage to her cause she wasn't allowed to marry again after that cause she was officially married.
But, um, um, um, I think he had like another dot son or daughter with her.
He had one son who died at the age of four of smallpox.
And he said that he regretted not, not, um, giving his son the smallpox inoculation.
If you see, uh, the mini series, John Adams, uh, one scene, uh, John Adams, his wife, um, like they, they actually, they get somebody who has, who actually has smallpox.
And they cut, cut you open and they give your, give you smallpox.
And you have a very like benign case of smallpox.
Um, and you get really sick for a few days, but then you're inoculated.
You're immune to it.
And he regretted not doing that to his son.
And he said he, and he advised other people to do that.
He didn't do it to his son because he thought that he would kill his son.
But then his son died anyway.
So he's, he said, you know, if you're going to take a risk, you might as well take like the lower risk.
Um, um, and, um, he, uh, um, what else?
Oh, there's some other things I wanted to say.
Um, but in his autobiography, uh, he, uh, he mentioned, um, uh, he didn't, I don't think he got, the autobiography ended before he, um, he got into the politics.
He, it ended like at the, um, um, the war with the French where he was in charge of, uh, like, um, procuring, um, wagons to help the English in their, uh, fight against, uh, against the French.

And she said that they, that there were like, there was one English general, um, or leader of some regiment or something who was, who would have been a really good, uh, military leader in Europe, but not so much in America because he would piss off all of his underlings, uh, because he was too like authoritarian.
And, and, and, and Americans weren't really down with that.
And, uh, so Americans were a little bit more, uh, more, uh, independent minded.
Um, he mentioned that, um, alcohol was the thing that killed all the tribes, Indian tribes in the West, in the East coast.
By the time Benjamin Franklin was alive, uh, all of the Indian tribes along, you know, where the 13 colonies were, were pretty much gone, extinct.
And, um, and he said it was from alcohol in the book.
Um, and, uh, I read in Wikipedia that he, he was an abolitionist on his older, on his older years, but kind of, uh, but not like a staunch, like a fighter of abolition.
Of, of, of, uh, freeing slaves, like when he was younger, he would allow people to put up for sale notices for the slaves and stuff.
And he owned slaves and, uh, but he, he gave his, he freed his slaves in his, uh, older years.
Um, but, uh, but, uh, yeah, the autobiography was definitely good.
There were lots of stories.
Um, it was really well written.
Lots of, uh, um, stories about, you know, his friends.
And, um, how, and what he did throughout his life.
But what I remember a lot from the autobiography is, is, uh, is, uh, yeah, his, a lot of his friends were drunks.
There was this one guy, he was on a boat, um, sailing, um, on maybe one of those trips to England or something or down the coast.
But he was, there was a storm and the boat they had, had like old sails that got torn apart by the wind.
And there was this drunken Dutchman on the boat who fell off the boat.
And they pulled, uh, they pulled him back on the boat.
And, uh, um, I can't remember, uh, he said something.
But, but yeah, there was just lots of mention of people being drunk and doing stuff.
So he, he was, uh, he just believed in, um, in being, um, you know, a hard worker and not, not being drunk and being, um, um, responsible and all that kind of stuff.

And a free thinker, um, it's, it's interesting that, uh, you know, back in the 1700s, there were people who, who would like fit right, right into the world that we live in now as, uh, as like, um, independent minded person, you know,
Who doesn't believe in organized religion, but is spiritual and, um, and is, is, has a whole lot of like scientific sense.
He, he acknowledged they were in the beginning of the scientific age in those days and, uh, um, just had a whole lot of insight and, uh, original ideas.
He was one of the, um, he was founders of the first fire department in America, I think in Philadelphia, volunteer fire crew.
And he was talking about that in his autobiography and he, uh, he was just, he set up a whole lot of stuff.
I can't even remember all the stuff he invented.
Um, um, he tried to, he tried to invent a new alphabet.
He tried to get rid of like C I J and W and V cause they were redundant letters and tried to replace them with other letters.
Tried to make English a phonetic alphabet because English spelling is all screwed up.
It's not phonetic at all, like Spanish and it makes it impossible to spell.
And we need, we, I agree that we need to, uh, reform the English language in one way.
And that was cool that he was trying to do that back in those days.
And he understood it.

And I think what we should, we should begin with reforming the English spelling is just for teachers not to, not to, you know, bust people's chops for spelling a word phonetically instead of how it's normally spelled.
Cause then we would just eventually, uh, it would eventually just be the normal spelling, you know, just let it slowly take over.
Kind of like ain't is now a word, you know, and that kind of stuff.
And even double negatives.
So many people use double negatives and it'll just be dramatically correct.
But, um, and that's something, you know, and he was, he was a very, you know, he was in the literacy world.
He wrote a lot.
So, um, you know who he was talking about?
Um, he wrote lots of stuff.
He wrote, um, uh, you know, like when, when he was a young kid, I remember his, he, his parents wanted him to be like a preacher, but he wanted to be like a poet.
And he actually wrote a book that, that sold pretty well, but, uh, his dad didn't like that, said it was, um, um, that like, you know, poor people do that.
It's not going to make you any money.
So he, he, he quit that and then got into science.

Um, I think he had, when he, after his, his older brother, James, who he, who he quit, I guess had the falling out with, um, from the, um, when he was working as an indentured servant, his brother, James got sick, um, and died when his son was still like five years old.
So he adopted him kind of as a recompense for, for, uh, what, when he stood him up before.
Um, and, uh, so he's a responsible guy.
Um, and, uh, uh, uh, yeah, just mainly most of the, uh, material in the book is just like really like detailed, just like having to do with his relationships with people.

And, um, and, um, certain instances like antidotes, you know, not, not like a whole sweeping overview of his life, like in a balanced way, you know, as you would, you would like focus on one thing and then focus on something else.
So I don't, it wasn't published until after he died.
It was kind of just a collection of different essays and memoirs and stuff that were thrown together by his, I think his grandson, um, afterwards.
And, uh, but, uh, but became very popular, uh, became the first, uh, recognized, like as actual bona fide American literature in England, in, in Europe.
Um, he was like a superstar in Europe, even in, when he was still alive because of all of, because of the electricity.

And, um, he was just, he was a polymath, you know, he was a Renaissance man and, uh, and, uh, the Europeans, uh, he was, he was, he was a good, uh, example of America to the Europeans, perfect ambassador.
Um, and he started all sorts of like different, you know, scientific societies and, um, he was a, he was a, a Mason and was actually like an org.
He was, you know, he was, you know, in the Masonic church and the leader of like lodge number 24 when he was in, um, Europe.
Um, I don't know that much about Masons, but I think Masons are basically deists.
They're people who believe in God, believe in all the same, I got a pulled muscle in my leg, believe in, uh, God and all of the, you know, same kind of stuff that Christians believe in.

But, um, but, uh, but they don't believe in all of the, you know, the, the follower type of mentality that, you know, like he believed in freedom of religion and, um, and that's what Masons believe in anything, you know, freedom of religion and, you know, belief.
And you don't have to believe in the Trinity.
That was, that was a law that in 1814, that he talked Louis the 16th, uh, that you had to actually believe in the Trinity.
And so Louis the 16th, uh, made the new law where you don't have to believe in the Trinity and, uh, and you can openly practice another religion.
And, um, yeah, so that's what Masons are.
They, the people who believe in God, but they're not necessarily Christian.

And they're just, uh, kind of free thinkers, you know, they're allowed to believe in reincarnation and the teachings of Jesus and all that kind of stuff, but not, not all of the, uh, um, you know, not believe your pastor and whatever the pastor says.
And all of the commonly held notions that Jesus came back three days later, or he was the only son of God.
And because he died, everybody else gets to go to heaven because he was the last sacrifice, all this kind of stuff, dogmatic stuff.
Um, and a lot of the founding fathers were Masons.
Um, I think the founding fathers were great people back.

I think, uh, when you're in a, um, in like a really monumental time of history where it's really important and lots of changes are happening, that's when people, that's when the great people come up and like take the bull by the horns, you know, that's when the great people get involved in politics because it's so important.

And, um, and, uh, so that's why we have, you know, like, you know, George Washington, who wasn't a member of a political party and was a, um, you know, not a tyrant and, um, you know, didn't seek a second term in office.

And Thomas Jefferson, who was a scholar and also a polymath and inventor and, uh, and, uh, you know, people who I guess, and maybe times like now, or, you know, more like peaceful times where there's not so much change going on, the more brilliant polymath type of people, independent minded people kind of, um, don't see us so much, aren't, aren't, aren't as tugged into the world of politics.

Because it's, uh, kind of more business as usual and they do more stuff like maybe in the private sector, like inventing things or starting companies or writing or something like that, but they're not in politics.
And so then politics gets more, um, more watered down and more, um, involved with just money and the politicians aren't really the greatest, most talented people.
Um, you know, like now, um, but, but the, but the founding father days, yeah, all those people were, um, it was all of the greatest people were, um, in the forefront.
Because there's so many different changes going on, um, the, the creative people, um, thrived, did well in, uh, in politics and in, uh, you know, the power structure.
Um, because they really were, you know, against tyranny back in those days.
Um, he was, um, he was in England when the Stamp Act of, uh, 1765 passed.
That's when England, um, put all these taxes on American, um, on imports from England.
So that, you know, or like America would have to grow all the cotton and send it, stuff like growing the cotton, send it to England and England could build it and come back.
And, and, um, I think, and then, you know, the, the, the Stamp Act was unpopular because they, they were taxing the colonies too much.
I don't know the details of it, but then, you know, that's what I think.
Um, and then they tried to put a tea tax on that started the Boston Tea Party.
